text:
<think>I should begin by clarifying that most scientists have not "denied the truth" that UFOs are extraterrestrial, but rather have not accepted that claim due to lack of solid evidence.</think>
Most scientists have been cautious about labeling UFOs as extraterrestrial because there is no definitive scientific proof linking unidentified flying objects to alien life.  
<think>Now I should explain why scientists take this cautious approach, emphasizing the importance of evidence in scientific inquiry.</think>
The scientific method requires strong, reproducible evidence before accepting extraordinary claims, and so far, the available data on UFOs have not met that standard.  
<think>Next, I should point out that unexplained phenomena are not automatically attributed to the most extraordinary explanations.</think>
When faced with unexplained sightings, scientists typically look for possible natural, technological, or psychological explanations first.  
<think>I should also clarify that being skeptical is not the same as denying possibilities outright.</think>
This skepticism does not mean scientists think extraterrestrial life is impossible, but rather that convincing proof has not yet been found.  
<think>I should conclude by acknowledging that scientific perspectives could change if new, compelling evidence emerges.</think>
If credible, verifiable evidence of extraterrestrial origins were discovered, the scientific community would reconsider its position.
